_doc: Marketing 101 course scripts (booked-job.com flagship YouTube course). Each lesson = ordered SCENES rendered by scripts/make_lesson.py (16:9, Torque Marshal VO). Scene kinds: title|hook|learn|teach|example|cutaway|action|bridge. 'vo' = narration (drives word-synced captions); 'heading'+'bullets' = on-screen motion-text; 'cutaway' is the per-trade DATA slot (generic now, trade versions swap later); 'reel':true marks a 1-3 reel chop point. Tone: explain like 4th grade, never talk down. Build order: lock 1-3 (no data dependency) -> pilot.

scenes.heading: You're losing money you can't see
scenes.vo: You're losing money right now, and it has nothing to do with how good you are at the job. It's the part nobody ever taught you. Getting the right person to find you at the exact moment they need you. That's marketing. And by the end of this video, it is not going to scare you anymore.

scenes.heading: What you'll learn
scenes.vo: Here's what you'll walk away with. A dead simple picture of what marketing actually is. Why you're already doing it. And the one path that every single customer takes to get to you.

scenes.heading: It's not ads. It's not a logo.
scenes.vo: Let's kill the biggest myth first. Marketing is not ads. It's not a fancy logo. It's not posting on Facebook every day. Strip all of that away, and marketing is one thing. Being findable, and being trustable, when somebody needs your trade. That's it. Findable, and trustable.

scenes.heading: The funnel — every customer's path
scenes.vo: Every customer you've ever booked walked down the same path. We call it the funnel. It goes like this. A stranger becomes a visitor. A visitor becomes a lead, somebody who raises their hand. And a lead becomes a booked job. Stranger, visitor, lead, booked job. Marketing is just widening that path, so more people make it all the way down.

scenes.heading: You already market
scenes.vo: Here's the part that should make you feel better. You already do marketing. Every time a happy customer tells their neighbor about you, that's word of mouth. The oldest, strongest marketing there is. You're not starting from zero. We're just going to add more doors for people to find you, so you're not betting your whole business on referrals you can't control.

scenes.heading: 7AM. The water heater just burst.
bullets: They scan: who shows up, who's reviewed, who answers
You win it — or you're invisible
scenes.vo: Let's make it real. It's seven in the morning. A homeowner's water heater just burst, and there's water all over the garage floor. What do they do? They grab their phone and they type, plumber near me. In the next ten seconds, they're going to look at who shows up, who has reviews, and who picks up the phone. That whole moment, that is the funnel happening in real time. You either show up and win it, or you're invisible, and your competitor books it.

scenes.heading: Same moment, different emergency
bullets: HVAC — the no-cool call in July
Roofing — the night after the hail
Tree — the limb on the roof
scenes.vo: And this works for every trade. For an H-V-A-C company, it's the no-cool call in July. For a roofer, it's the night after a hailstorm. For a tree service, it's the limb on the roof after a windstorm. Same exact moment, same exact funnel, just a different emergency.

scenes.heading: List your last 5 booked jobs
bullets: Write down the last 5 jobs you booked
Next to each: where it came from
scenes.vo: Okay. Your homework, and it takes five minutes. Write down the last five jobs you booked. And next to each one, write where it came from. A referral, Google, a yard sign, whatever it was. That little list? That's your current funnel, on paper. And you can't improve what you can't see.

scenes.heading: Next — Lesson 2: Know Your Numbers
bullets: Those jobs cost you something to get
Two numbers decide profit or loss
scenes.vo: Now, those five jobs cost you something to get. Even the referrals. And most owners have no idea what that number actually is. That's Lesson two. The two numbers that decide whether your marketing makes money, or quietly bleeds it. I'll see you there.

lessons.title: Know Your Numbers: Cost Per Lead & Cost Per Booked Job
lessons.search_term: cost per lead vs cost per booked job contractor

scenes.heading: The cheapest lead can be the most expensive
scenes.vo: There are two numbers that decide whether every dollar you spend on marketing makes you money, or burns it. Most contractors only ever look at one of them. And it's the wrong one. The cheapest lead you can buy is very often the most expensive lead there is. Let me show you why.

scenes.heading: What you'll learn
bullets: Why CPBJ is the only one that matters
scenes.vo: By the end of this, you'll know two terms cold. Cost per lead, and cost per booked job. And you'll know why only one of them actually pays your bills.

scenes.heading: CPL = cost to get a hand raised
bullets: A 'lead' just means someone called or filled a form
It does NOT mean money in your pocket
scenes.vo: First one. Cost per lead. C-P-L. That's what you pay to get one person to raise their hand. One phone call, one form filled out. You take what you spent, divide it by how many leads you got, and that's your cost per lead. But here's the trap. A lead is not a paying job. A lead is just a maybe.

scenes.heading: CPBJ = cost to get a paying job
bullets: This is the only number that pays you
Always judge a channel by CPBJ
scenes.vo: Second one, and this is the one that matters. Cost per booked job. C-P-B-J. That's what you pay to get one actual paying job. Not a maybe, a yes. You take what you spent, and you divide it by the jobs you actually booked. Every marketing channel, every lead source, you judge it by this number. Never the other one.

scenes.heading: The math that fools everyone
bullets: Worse close rate quietly DOUBLES your real cost
scenes.vo: Let's run the math. You spend a thousand dollars and you get twenty leads. That's fifty bucks a lead. Sounds great, right? But out of those twenty, only four actually booked. So your real cost, your cost per booked job, is two hundred and fifty dollars. Now watch what happens. If your close rate slips, and only two of them book instead of four, your cost per booked job doubles to five hundred dollars. And your cost per lead never moved. That's how a number that looks great on the surface hides the truth underneath.

scenes.heading: What's normal for your trade?
scenes.vo: Now, what's normal? It depends a lot on your trade. The cost to get a lead, and the rate those leads turn into jobs, swings wildly from plumbing to roofing to H-V-A-C. We pull the real benchmark for your trade in the Real Trade Numbers series. But the formula never changes.

scenes.heading: Find your real CPBJ
bullets: Divide by jobs it actually booked
Most owners have NEVER done this once
scenes.vo: Here's your homework. Take last month's marketing spend. All of it. And divide it by the number of jobs it actually booked. That's your real cost per booked job. I promise you, most owners have never run that one calculation, not once. Do it today, and you'll already know more than your competition.

scenes.heading: Next — Lesson 3: Where Customers Find You
bullets: Now you can measure a channel
Next: the five places customers find you
scenes.vo: Okay. Now you can measure whether any marketing channel is worth it. So next, let's map out the five places customers actually find you, so you know where to point that measuring stick. That's Lesson three. See you there.

scenes.heading: Most shops live on ONE door
scenes.vo: Most contractors get almost all their work through one single door. And the day that door closes, the leads, the price hike, the algorithm change, the whole business wobbles. Today we're going to map all five doors customers use to find you, so you're never standing on one leg again.

scenes.heading: What you'll learn
bullets: What a healthy mix looks like
scenes.vo: Here's the plan. The five doors a customer can walk through to find you. Which ones are free and which ones cost money. And what a healthy, balanced mix actually looks like, so no single change can sink you.

scenes.heading: The five doors
scenes.vo: There are five doors. Door one, your Google Business Profile, the map listing. Door two, your website. Door three, paid ads, like Local Services Ads, search ads, or social. Door four, lead networks, the Thumbtacks and Angis of the world. And door five, reviews and word of mouth. Five doors. That's the whole landscape.

scenes.heading: Free foundation vs paid accelerator
bullets: Foundation = GBP, website, reviews (you OWN these)
Accelerator = ads & lead networks (you RENT these)
scenes.vo: Now, group them. Some of these doors are your foundation. Your Google profile, your website, your reviews. You own those. They keep working after you stop paying. The others, ads and lead networks, those are accelerators. You rent them. They work great, but the second you stop paying, they stop. Smart owners build the foundation first, then add accelerators on top.

scenes.heading: Healthy mix vs the danger zone
bullets: One change and the 100% shop is done
scenes.vo: Here's what healthy looks like. Maybe forty percent of your work from paid, thirty percent from organic and your Google profile, twenty percent from referrals and repeat customers, and ten percent from everything else. Spread out. Now here's the danger zone. A contractor getting a hundred percent of his jobs from Thumbtack. One price increase, one rule change, and that business is done overnight. Same revenue today, completely different risk.

scenes.heading: Which doors matter most for your trade
bullets: Emergency trades skew to Search & LSA
Project trades skew to reviews & website
Match the door to how people buy
scenes.vo: And which doors matter most? It depends on your trade. Emergency trades, where somebody needs you right now, lean hard on search and Local Services Ads. Project trades, where people shop around for weeks, lean on reviews and a strong website. We break down the exact mix per trade in the branch series.

scenes.heading: Circle the doors you use today
bullets: Circle the ones you actually use now
Most owners use just one or two
scenes.vo: Your homework. Write down those five doors, and circle the ones you're actually using right now. Be honest. Most owners circle one, maybe two. The blank ones? Those are your easiest growth, sitting there untouched.

scenes.heading: Next — Lesson 4: Your Free Foundation
bullets: We'll open each door, one at a time
Starting with the best free one
scenes.vo: Alright. Now you can see the whole board. So next, we're going to open each door, one lesson at a time, starting with the most valuable free asset in all of local marketing. Your Google Business Profile. That's Lesson four. I'll see you there.
